text: fluorescence angiography during transanal transtomal proctectomy and ileal pouch anal anastomosis. A sixteen year old patient diagnosed with ulcerative colitis who underwent a subtotal colectomy one year before was scheduled for a completion proctectomy and pouch construction. This is a demonstration of our technique of double single port transanal transtomal proctectomy with IPAA with fluorescence-guided perfusion assessment.

The surgeon begins the procedure by mobilizing the stoma and resecting the iliocutaneous suture using a linear stapler. The last ileal loop is returned into the abdomen, and a neomarconium is created with a single-port device. Adhesions from previous surgery are lysed to enhance bowel mobility.

The surgeon prepares and exposes the mesenteric pedicle, isolating the mesenteric axis at its origin. The most dependent ileal loop is stretched toward the pelvis to assess its length. The Cochrane maneuver is performed to increase the reach of the future pouch.

The surgeon exteriorizes the last ileal loop intended for pouch construction. Using transillumination, the ileocolic vessels are identified and clamped at their origin. Indocyanine green is injected intravenously, and real-time angiography is performed to assess bubble perfusion. If additional length is required, the ileocolic vessels are sectioned following a fluorescent check for adequate residual perfusion.

The surgeon assesses the reach of the ileal pouch using the inferior aspect of the symphysis pubis as a reference. The pouch is constructed with two fires of a 100mm linear stapler. The patency of the ileal pouch is confirmed by inflating it with 300cc of saline solution, followed by a reassessment of its vascularization.

The surgeon creates a purse string suture at the apex of the ileal pouch using Proline Zero. A 31-millimeter anvil is inserted into the pouch, and a 19 French Redon drain is positioned at the tip of the anvil.

Two surgical teams perform a proctectomy transanally and transtomally. An anal retractor is placed, and a pudendal block is administered. A purse-string suture is created 3 cm from the dentate line using a circular anal dilator. Transabdominally, the rectum is mobilized, and the inferior mesenteric pedicle is isolated. A stable pneumo-rectum is maintained with a single-axis transanal device and a smoke evacuator. Circumferential rectotomy is completed.

The transanal team initiates a bad total mesorectal excision (TME) to minimize nerve damage, while the abdominal team sections the inferior mesenteric vessels. Transillumination is used to connect the superior and inferior dissection planes. The transabdominal dissection continues until it meets the transanal dissection, completing the full mobilization of the rectum.

The surgical specimen is extracted through the transtomal single-port device. A laparoscopic grasper is used to pull the tubular drain placed at the tip of the anvil, transposing the ileal pouch into the pelvic cavity. The surgeon checks the correct orientation of the pouch within the pelvic cavity.

The surgeon places a purse-string suture at the distal rectal cuff transanally. The anvil is pulled through the distal rectal cuff, and the distal purse-string suture is tied around the anvil. A 31mm silk cord stapler is used to create the ileal pouch anastomosis, and the integrity of the donuts is checked. The anastomosis is evaluated transanally using fluorescence angiography.

The surgeon creates a diverting ileostomy, visually establishing the stoma site and securing the bowel segment. The surgical field shows the completed ileostomy with surrounding tissue and instruments used in the procedure.
